0: Choosing co-substrates to supplement biogas production from animal slurry - A life cycle assessment of the environmental consequences
Giovanna Catalina Croxatto Vega Marieke ten Hoeve Morten Birkved b Sven G. Sommer c, Sander Bruun a,*

<list>a Department of Plant and Environmental Sciences, University of Copenhagen, Thorvaldsensvej 40, 1871 Frederiksberg C, Denmark 
b Department of Management Engineering, Technical University of Denmark, Produktionstorvet, Building 424, 2800 Kongens Lyngby, Denmark

<list>Digestion resulted in reduced marine eutrophication and fossil depletion potential. 
Digestion of straw, otherwise left on field, had the lowest environmental impact. 
Diverting the co-substrates from incineration to biogas production increased impacts. 
Results appear most sensitive to the factor for N20 emission from the field.</list>
2: Article history: Received 11 July 2014 Received in revised form 21 August 2014 Accepted 23 August 2014 Available online 2 September 2014
Keywords: Biogas Straw Organic fraction household waste Separated slurry Life cycle assessment
3: Biogas production from animal slurry can provide substantial contributions to reach renewable energy targets, yet due to the low methane potential of slurry, biogas plants depend on the addition of co-substrates to make operations profitable. The environmental performance of three underexploited co-substrates, straw, organic household waste and the solid fraction of separated slurry, were assessed against slurry management without biogas production, using LCA methodology. The analysis showed straw, which would have been left on arable fields, to be an environmentally superior co-substrate. Due to its low nutrient content and high methane potential, straw yields the lowest impacts for eutrophi- cation and the highest climate change and fossil depletion savings. Co-substrates diverted from incineration to biogas production had fewer environmental benefits, due to the loss of energy production, which is then produced from conventional fossil fuels. The scenarios can often provide benefits for one impact category while causing impacts in another.
4: The sustainability of bioenergy production depends largely on the biomass resource and technology used. For this reason, bioen- ergy production can result in different environmental efficiencies

and in some cases even lead to more environmental damage than savings. In Europe, bioenergy has been identified as a key factor for meeting the goal of 20% renewable energy by 2020 (European Parliament and the Council, 2009). Careful consideration to the manner in which bioenergy should develop has been given in a ser- ies of reports by the European Environment Agency, so that envi- ronmental impacts are reduced and greenhouse gas (GHG) mitigation is maximized (EEA, 2013). More specifically, it has been found that bioenergy, from waste and agricultural residues, pro- vides substantial advantages for GHG balances and reductions of ecosystem impacts. Further the current energy cropping trends (1st and 2nd generation) are not immediately environmentally beneficial, may contribute to food scarcity (EEA, 2013) and/or accelerate global warming (Liska et al., 2014). In this context, bio- gas production is environmentally favorable only when appropri- ate biomass resources are employed.

A large expansion of the biogas sector has been prompted by the above mentioned EU targets, resulting in a growth of 18.2% in biogas electricity production in 2011, equivalent to 424 PJ of pri- mary energy production in the EU. In Germany, the leading nation in terms of biogas production, biogas accounts for 3% of primary energy consumption and an impressive 1310 new biogas plants were commissioned in 2010 alone (EurObserv'ER, 2012). In Den- mark, the latest energy strategy announced a commitment to become a fossil fuel free society by 2050 (Regeringen, 2011). Sev- eral subsidies have been introduced since, to further develop the biogas sector. Among them, a subsidy of 3 euro per GJ of biogas produced from animal manure is in line with the resource effi- ciency framework that the EU is establishing and supports the ambitious goal to utilize 50% of animal manure for the production of biogas by 2020 (EEA, 2013; Regeringen, 2011). This signifies a substantial increase from the current utilization of this resource in Denmark, which today amounts to 5-7% utilization of the avail- able manure for biogas production (Birkmose et al., 2013). Future increments in biogas production capacity are dependent on the deployment of large quantities of substrates for biogas purposes, of which manure could provide a substantial quantity.

Animal manure has the potential to produce 827 PJ of biogas based energy in the EU (Birkmose et al., 2007). However, when slurry is digested alone, methane (CH4) yields are low and conse- quently, it is economically unfeasible for biogas plants to run solely on manure (Angelidaki and Ellegaard, 2003). Co-substrates such as slaughterhouse waste and food industry wastes, which contribute with easily degradable organic matter, are used to increase meth- ane yields. However, these resources are nearly fully utilized so that biogas plants are forced to look for alternative co-substrates (Birkmose et al., 2013). The use of energy crops, which has increased dramatically in recent years, has been one option used in the biogas industry (Meyer-Aurich et al., 2012). However, this is undesirable as arable land is a scarce resource and energy crops compete for land with food production. Further expansion of the biogas sector in the EU should preferably happen with co-sub- strates that do not compromise food security and/or trigger land use changes (EEA, 2013). Examples of such co-substrates are wheat straw, the organic fraction of household waste and the solid frac- tion of separated slurry. These co-substrates have been shown to add much value to biogas operations in the form of increased methane yields and nutrient recovery from digestate (Davidsson et al., 2007; Hjorth et al., 2011; Moller et al., 2007).

Recent LCAs of biogas production have evaluated many differ- ent aspects, such as the GHG mitigation potential and concerns for land use change (Meyer-Aurich et al., 2012), digestate use and energy crops, among others (Laurent et al., 2014). Few have focused on the environmental effects of using different, readily- available, waste products as co-substrates for biogas production (Hamelin et al., 2014, 2011). The objective of this study is to ana- lyze the environmental implications of using wheat straw, the organic fraction of household waste (OW) and the solid fraction (SF) of slurry as co-substrates to slurry, using the LCA methodology.

8: The present LCA conforms to the standards outlined in ISO 14040 and ISO 14044 (ISO, 2006a,b). The study analyzed the con- sequences arising from the choice to co-digest manure with the co-substrates and the system was hence expanded in accordance with the new choices i.e. to divert the co-substrates from their con- ventional uses. Marginal energy and mineral fertilizer suppliers have been identified and allocation was avoided when possible and feasible.

The functional unit (FU) to which all inputs and outputs of the study are related is the contemporary management of 1000 kg of pig slurry post-excretion under Danish conditions. This ensures there is an equal basis for the comparison of the different manure management scenarios.

<list>Baseline: manure is excreted by fattening pigs in housing units with partially-slated floors. The slurry is pumped to an outdoor slurry storage, where it awaits field application in the spring. Upon field application mineral nitrogen fertilizer is substituted. This constitutes conventional manure management in Denmark. 
Biogas scenarios: After slurry excretion in housing units, slurry is pumped to an outdoor pre-storage where it awaits transport to the biogas plant. Slurry is processed in the biogas plant where biogas and digestate are produced. The biogas is upgraded to biomethane quality by removing carbon dioxide (CO2) and is then injected into the natural gas grid, substituting natural gas. The digestate is transported to the farm to an outdoor stor- age, where it awaits application to arable fields in the spring. Upon field application mineral nitrogen fertilizer is substituted. The four biogas scenarios are: 
o St-field: pig slurry is co-digested with extruded wheat straw that would have otherwise been ploughed into the fields. After co-digestion, all biogas scenarios follow the steps described above. 
o St-burn: pig slurry is co-digested with wheat straw that would have otherwise been incinerated for electricity pro- duction. Electricity production by power plants incinerat- ing straw is avoided and must be substituted by the electricity production resource that will be affected by the use of biogas for power and heat production in the Dan- ish market; namely electricity and heat production from coal. 
o OW: pig slurry is co-digested with organic household waste that would have otherwise been incinerated for electricity production. Electricity production is avoided and must be substituted as in St-burn. 
o Sep: pig slurry is co-digested with the solid fraction of solid-liquid separated slurry. The solid fraction (SF) would have otherwise been transported to a recipient farm in need of organic fertilizers, far away from the animal farm. Due to biogas production, the SF is not applied on the fields of the recipient farm and instead mineral fertilizer is applied in this farm.</list>

The software Gabi 6.0 by PE International was used to carry out the assessment. Generic processes, such as electricity production, mineral fertilizer production and transport vehicles, were taken from the 6.106 database. In addition, Gabi processes were used for incineration of straw and of organic household waste. The emissions from slurry and digestate were analyzed from the moment the slurry was excreted by pigs until 20 years after field application, in accordance with the FARM-N tool used to model these emissions (Hutchings et al., 2013). The effects of emissions i.e. CO2, N20 in the environment were assessed for a 100 year per- iod, in accordance with the ReCiPe hierarchist method (Goedkoop et al., 2009).

There are currently no guidelines provided by the ISO on how to include biogenic C into LCA studies. Furthermore, there are several authors that advice to treat biogenic C in different ways as summa- rized in ten Hoeve et al. (2014). In order to avoid introducing sub- jective decisions on the nature of carbon emissions, it was decided
to include biogenic C in the assessment. However, results exclud- ing biogenic carbon were also analyzed for comparative purposes.

Capital goods e.g. construction of the biogas plant, were not included in the assessments for new biogas plants, as this will not vary depending on the choice of co-substrate. Also, the full effects on toxicity of applying manure products were not assessed i.e. Cu and Zn in the manure, but it could only be significant for the ow scenario, since the other co-substrates are currently applied on arable fields whether or not they are used for biogas production. Also, it is expected that the strict Danish laws are effective against heavy metal pollution of soils (Ministry of Food, Agriculture and Fisheries, 2009).

Biogas scenarios: After slurry excretion in housing units, slurry is pumped to an outdoor pre-storage where it awaits transport to the biogas plant. Slurry is processed in the biogas plant where biogas and digestate are produced. The biogas is upgraded to biomethane quality by removing carbon dioxide (CO2) and is then injected into the natural gas grid, substituting natural gas. The digestate is transported to the farm to an outdoor stor- age, where it awaits application to arable fields in the spring. Upon field application mineral nitrogen fertilizer is substituted. The four biogas scenarios are: 
o St-field: pig slurry is co-digested with extruded wheat straw that would have otherwise been ploughed into the fields. After co-digestion, all biogas scenarios follow the steps described above. 
o St-burn: pig slurry is co-digested with wheat straw that would have otherwise been incinerated for electricity pro- duction. Electricity production by power plants incinerat- ing straw is avoided and must be substituted by the electricity production resource that will be affected by the use of biogas for power and heat production in the Dan- ish market; namely electricity and heat production from coal. 
o OW: pig slurry is co-digested with organic household waste that would have otherwise been incinerated for electricity production. Electricity production is avoided and must be substituted as in St-burn. 
o Sep: pig slurry is co-digested with the solid fraction of solid-liquid separated slurry. The solid fraction (SF) would have otherwise been transported to a recipient farm in need of organic fertilizers, far away from the animal farm. Due to biogas production, the SF is not applied on the fields of the recipient farm and instead mineral fertilizer is applied in this farm.</list>

The software Gabi 6.0 by PE International was used to carry out the assessment. Generic processes, such as electricity production, mineral fertilizer production and transport vehicles, were taken from the 6.106 database. In addition, Gabi processes were used for incineration of straw and of organic household waste. The emissions from slurry and digestate were analyzed from the moment the slurry was excreted by pigs until 20 years after field application, in accordance with the FARM-N tool used to model these emissions (Hutchings et al., 2013). The effects of emissions i.e. CO2, N20 in the environment were assessed for a 100 year per- iod, in accordance with the ReCiPe hierarchist method (Goedkoop et al., 2009).

There are currently no guidelines provided by the ISO on how to include biogenic C into LCA studies. Furthermore, there are several authors that advice to treat biogenic C in different ways as summa- rized in ten Hoeve et al. (2014). In order to avoid introducing sub- jective decisions on the nature of carbon emissions, it was decided
to include biogenic C in the assessment. However, results exclud- ing biogenic carbon were also analyzed for comparative purposes.

Capital goods e.g. construction of the biogas plant, were not included in the assessments for new biogas plants, as this will not vary depending on the choice of co-substrate. Also, the full effects on toxicity of applying manure products were not assessed i.e. Cu and Zn in the manure, but it could only be significant for the ow scenario, since the other co-substrates are currently applied on arable fields whether or not they are used for biogas production. Also, it is expected that the strict Danish laws are effective against heavy metal pollution of soils (Ministry of Food, Agriculture and Fisheries, 2009).
12: The geographical scope of this study is Denmark. Thus, all data in the study including: slurry chemical composition, emission fac- tors, biogas plant operational conditions, crop rotation, soil profiles and temperatures, were chosen to be representative of Denmark. However, important processes that occur outside of Denmark, such as mineral fertilizer production, are also included in the model. Danish laws that regulate manure management, have been fol- lowed and modeled agricultural/industrial practices are either cur- rently in place or potentially relevant. The results of this study are, due to the site-specific nature of the assessment, not applicable for other geographical areas, without extensive adjustments to the data.
13: The boundary for the system starts at excretion of slurry in ani- mal houses and ends after slurry or digestate are applied to arable fields (Fig. 1). Electricity, heat and fuel consumption, as well as transport are included. Pig production is not included, as produc- tion of biogas from slurry is not expected to cause any foreseeable

changes in this regard. Likewise, as the co-substrates to slurry are all wastes, their production was also excluded; though it is acknowledged that their use in biogas could eventually lead to a rise in competition and changes in prices (Birkmose et al., 2013). The model includes activities that are avoided when conventional use of the co-substrates does not occur. These activities, which will be referred to as the 'scenario specific processes', can be identified in Fig. 1 for each co-substrate.
14: In the baseline scenario, 1000 kg of slurry obtained from fatten- ing pigs is pumped after about 6 weeks to an outside storage tank at the animal farm, where it stays for an average of 6 months and is applied to arable fields in the spring. The application of slurry to the fields of the animal farm, results in the complete avoidance of application and production of ammonium nitrate, the marginal mineral N fertilizer. Slurry was assumed to have a mineral fertilizer efficiency (MFE) of 75%, based on the Danish Ministry of Food, Agri- culture and Fisheries, which sets MFE requirements for different organic fertilizer (Ministry of Food, Agriculture and Fisheries, 2009). Unlike similar LCAs, the application of slurry to animal farm fields is not assumed to avoid P and K mineral fertilizers. The rea- son for this is that yearly application of animal manures to the soils of animal farms, has led to a local accumulation of P and K, which means that there is no need for these fertilizers (Hansen et al., 2006b). Additionally, the EU Nitrates Directive has resulted in an almost optimal P and K balance at these farms, where farmers will most typically not apply P and K mineral fertilizer. Calculations for the baseline scenario were performed with the FARM-N model and follow the assumption in Hutchings et al. (2013), with a few excep- tions during storage (Table 2). Nitrogen in slurry was applied at a rate of 170 kg N ha yr-1, based on the Nitrates Directive, and P at a rate of 21.5 kg ha yr for the crop used in FARM-N

23: In the biogas scenarios, the reference slurry is pre-stored for 10 days at the animal farm, before it is transported to the biogas plant for co-digestion with a pre-treated substrate. In order to equalize the biogas scenarios, it was decided that an equal amount

of co-substrate total solids (TS), 53.5 kg, would be added to each scenario. After co-digestion, the slurry/co-substrate mixture (the digestate) follows the same fate as the reference scenario, with storage at the animal farm and subsequent application to the field. Transformations of N and P in the field after digestate application are again based on Hutchings et al. (2013) and the MFE of digestate is 80% (Ministry of Food, Agriculture and Fisheries, 2009). Trans- port distances between the co-substrates originating in the city and traveling to the animal farms were assumed to be 40 km, while transport from animal farms to the biogas plant was around 6 km (Birkmose et al., 2013).

2.3.2.1. Straw scenarios. In the straw scenarios, wheat straw is extruded before it is co-digested with the reference slurry. Extru- sion increases the biomethane potential (BMP) of straw by 70% (Hjorth et al., 2011). An amount of 5% extruded straw per slurry wet weight was added to the biogas reactor, based on the fact that Danish biogas plants are designed for pumpable substrates, which can tolerate a maximum of 10% untreated straw (Birkmose et al., 2013). A conservative amount of 5% treated straw corresponds to 53.5 kg of straw TS, which became the limiting factor for TS addi- tion in all biogas scenarios. Since straw is diverted from its conven- tional uses, it is then necessary to include the actions that did not occur, the scenario specific processes. In the case St-field, it was assumed that the straw would have otherwise been left on the fields, which was based on the large yearly surplus of straw in Den- mark, on average 2.1 million tons (38% of total) after all other uses such as, incineration, pellets, bedding and fodder (Birkmose et al., 2013).

As scenario specific processes proved to be crucial in a pre- assessment of the results, it was then decided that an additional scenario, St-burn, should include straw that would have otherwise been incinerated. Thus, in St-burn, the scenario specific processes are incineration of straw and the associated electricity and heat production. Consequently, the system is expanded to produce elec- tricity and heat from the marginal sources. In this case, allocation between electricity and heat was based on exergy, i.e. the maxi- mum work performed when a system falls into equilibrium with a heat reservoir, so that a sole output of electricity had to be substi- tuted. Thus, avoided incineration of straw, resulted in induced electricity production from hard coal. A value of TS was assumed to be the lower heating value (LHV) of the straw avoiding incineration. The waste-to-energy plant had a net effi- ciency of 38%, with electricity and heat efficiencies of 28% and 72%, respectively. Furthermore, the Gabi process "Wood (natural) in municipal waste incinerator" was used for the incineration of straw, which was scaled accordingly so that the TS content would match the TS content modeled.

2.3.2.2. Organic fraction of household waste scenario. The biopulping process, designed by Komtek Miljo A/S, to remove non-organic impurities from source sorted household waste, was modeled as pre-treatment for the waste in this scenario. This process removes most reject materials, i.e. plastic, glass, metal etc., resulting in a biopulp with 0.1% reject material (personal communication, Bitten Lorentzen, 05-07-2012). A quantity of 382 kg ww-superscript(1) of biopulp was added as co-substrate to the reference slurry, in order to attain 53.5 kg TS. In Denmark, most of the municipal waste is incinerated for energy production, so the scenario specific processes for the ow scenario are avoided incineration of this waste and associated induced marginal electricity production. The same waste-to- energy plant and procedure as in St-burn were used to handle the production of marginal electricity in this scenario. A value of 19 MJ kg-1 TS was assumed as the starting LHV, which becomes 2.7 MJ kg-1 waste considering that the ow was assumed to be 14% TS. The avoided incineration was modeled with the PE process

"Waste incineration (municipal waste)", which was considered the best solution, even though this process includes the incineration of the non-organic fraction of municipal waste. The PE process was scaled to match the correct TS of the assumed waste.

2.3.2.3. Separation scenario. In Sep, slurry was separated in order to obtain a solid fraction with a TS content of 53.5 kg for biogas pro- duction. The separation was carried out with a screw press and it was assumed that the SF, which is used for biogas production, comes from a farmer who is already separating slurry in order to avoid accumulation of macronutrients on his land. The farmer then supplies organic fertilizers to plant farms in need of these nutrients 100 km away. Thus, by being sent to the biogas plant, long-term storage of the SF is avoided, as well as subsequent application of it at the recipient farm in the spring. The recipient farm is then forced to apply marginal NPK mineral fertilizers, ammonium nitrate, triple superphosphate and potassium chloride, as it does not receive these nutrients from the SF. Therefore, the scenario specific processes for this scenario include avoided storage and application of the SF and application of mineral NPK fertilizers at the recipient farm. The impacts from application of the liquid frac- tion (LF) remaining after separation of slurry are not included in the assessment, as the fate of the SF does not induce a change in the use of the LF, which will be applied in the animal farm in any case. The SF replaces ammonium nitrate with an efficiency of 65% (Ministry of Food, Agriculture and Fisheries, 2009), while P and K are most commonly assumed to be replaced on a one to one ratio (ten Hoeve et al., 2014).

"Waste incineration (municipal waste)", which was considered the best solution, even though this process includes the incineration of the non-organic fraction of municipal waste. The PE process was scaled to match the correct TS of the assumed waste.

2.3.2.3. Separation scenario. In Sep, slurry was separated in order to obtain a solid fraction with a TS content of 53.5 kg for biogas pro- duction. The separation was carried out with a screw press and it was assumed that the SF, which is used for biogas production, comes from a farmer who is already separating slurry in order to avoid accumulation of macronutrients on his land. The farmer then supplies organic fertilizers to plant farms in need of these nutrients 100 km away. Thus, by being sent to the biogas plant, long-term storage of the SF is avoided, as well as subsequent application of it at the recipient farm in the spring. The recipient farm is then forced to apply marginal NPK mineral fertilizers, ammonium nitrate, triple superphosphate and potassium chloride, as it does not receive these nutrients from the SF. Therefore, the scenario specific processes for this scenario include avoided storage and application of the SF and application of mineral NPK fertilizers at the recipient farm. The impacts from application of the liquid frac- tion (LF) remaining after separation of slurry are not included in the assessment, as the fate of the SF does not induce a change in the use of the LF, which will be applied in the animal farm in any case. The SF replaces ammonium nitrate with an efficiency of 65% (Ministry of Food, Agriculture and Fisheries, 2009), while P and K are most commonly assumed to be replaced on a one to one ratio (ten Hoeve et al., 2014).
27: In the biogas scenarios, the reference slurry is pre-stored for 10 days at the animal farm, before it is transported to the biogas plant for co-digestion with a pre-treated substrate. In order to equalize the biogas scenarios, it was decided that an equal amount

of co-substrate total solids (TS), 53.5 kg, would be added to each scenario. After co-digestion, the slurry/co-substrate mixture (the digestate) follows the same fate as the reference scenario, with storage at the animal farm and subsequent application to the field. Transformations of N and P in the field after digestate application are again based on Hutchings et al. (2013) and the MFE of digestate is 80% (Ministry of Food, Agriculture and Fisheries, 2009). Trans- port distances between the co-substrates originating in the city and traveling to the animal farms were assumed to be 40 km, while transport from animal farms to the biogas plant was around 6 km (Birkmose et al., 2013).

2.3.2.1. Straw scenarios. In the straw scenarios, wheat straw is extruded before it is co-digested with the reference slurry. Extru- sion increases the biomethane potential (BMP) of straw by 70% (Hjorth et al., 2011). An amount of 5% extruded straw per slurry wet weight was added to the biogas reactor, based on the fact that Danish biogas plants are designed for pumpable substrates, which can tolerate a maximum of 10% untreated straw (Birkmose et al., 2013). A conservative amount of 5% treated straw corresponds to 53.5 kg of straw TS, which became the limiting factor for TS addi- tion in all biogas scenarios. Since straw is diverted from its conven- tional uses, it is then necessary to include the actions that did not occur, the scenario specific processes. In the case St-field, it was assumed that the straw would have otherwise been left on the fields, which was based on the large yearly surplus of straw in Den- mark, on average 2.1 million tons (38% of total) after all other uses such as, incineration, pellets, bedding and fodder (Birkmose et al., 2013).

As scenario specific processes proved to be crucial in a pre- assessment of the results, it was then decided that an additional scenario, St-burn, should include straw that would have otherwise been incinerated. Thus, in St-burn, the scenario specific processes are incineration of straw and the associated electricity and heat production. Consequently, the system is expanded to produce elec- tricity and heat from the marginal sources. In this case, allocation between electricity and heat was based on exergy, i.e. the maxi- mum work performed when a system falls into equilibrium with a heat reservoir, so that a sole output of electricity had to be substi- tuted. Thus, avoided incineration of straw, resulted in induced electricity production from hard coal. A value of TS was assumed to be the lower heating value (LHV) of the straw avoiding incineration. The waste-to-energy plant had a net effi- ciency of 38%, with electricity and heat efficiencies of 28% and 72%, respectively. Furthermore, the Gabi process "Wood (natural) in municipal waste incinerator" was used for the incineration of straw, which was scaled accordingly so that the TS content would match the TS content modeled.

29: Anaerobic digestion (AD) of the reference slurry and co-sub- strates was assumed to take place in a thermophilic centralized biogas plant, with a hydraulic retention time (HRT) of 15 days, which is common practice in these facilities (Hansen et al., 2006a). The biogas produced was assumed to contain 62% meth- ane, 33% carbon dioxide, and 1.5% other gases, such as hydrogen sulfide, hydrogen gas, water vapor, and ammonia. Biogas was also captured from digestate storage at the biogas plant, were an addi- tional 10% of the methane potential entering the plant was recov- ered (Luostarinen et al., 2011). Electricity consumption of the biogas reactor was 0.09 and heat consumption was cal- culated following the procedure in Hamelin et al. (2011). Marginal heat for the reactor was produced from natural gas provided from the grid. A heating value of 9.94 kWh m for CH4 was used to cal- culate the energy content of the upgraded biogas, with 96% meth- ane content (Hamelin et al., 2011). The produced biogas was assumed to be upgraded and exported to the natural gas grid, causing avoidance of natural gas extraction. The most commonly used technologies for upgrading of biogas, water scrubber and pressure swing adsorption (PSA), were used in this study. Fugitive CH4 emissions from the reactor and upgrading process were assumed to be 2.7% and 1.6% of the produced CH4, respectively, based on industry data and measurements (Luostarinen et al., 2011).

33: Several uncertain parameters were varied in the different life cycle stages, within their estimated uncertainty range, in order to test the robustness of the model's predictions. A reasonable range for the biomethane potentials of the co-substrates was used to test uncertainties related to biogas production. The effects of the uncer- tainties relating to NH3 emissions after slurry and digestate appli- cation in the field, which could be higher for digestate, were tested, as well as N20 emissions in the field. The effects of uncertainties relating to fugitive CH4 emissions from AD reactors and upgrading facilities, which have only been quantified on very few occasions, were also tested. Finally, substitution of marginal P and K mineral fertilizers, which was initially excluded, was included to test how this affected the results. The uncertainty ranges used in the analy- sis are shown in Table 3.
34: The environmental impact potentials within the most relevant impact categories, through each life cycle stage, are shown in Fig. 2 for all scenarios. The net impact can be obtained by subtract- ing environmental savings (negative bars) from burdens (positive bars).

The biogas scenarios performed decisively better than the base- line for the FD and ME impact categories, while for the remaining categories, the performance against the baseline depended on the co-substrate used. FD results showed the largest net benefits for the straw scenarios, which substituted the largest quantity of nat- ural gas. At the same time, fossil fuel consumption in St-burn for the avoided incineration of straw and subsequently induced pro- duction of marginal electricity, lead to considerable losses to these benefits. The same was the case for ow and the avoided incinera-

tion of waste therein, which together with higher fuel consump- tion for heating the digester and pre-treatment of this co-substrate, made the ow scenario the least preferable of the biogas scenarios in terms of avoided FD. Moderate savings in the Sep scenario reflect the low rate of natural gas substitution arising from this co-substrate's low methane potential. However, the remaining life cycle of Sep did not induce any major fossil fuel con- sumption, which means that this scenario performs better than OW, regardless of low methane yields. There were large savings for ME of 205% for St-field, 130% for St-burn, 138% for OW, and 135% for Sep, in comparison to the baseline. In this category, the most important impacts occur in the field from NO3 leaching and to a smaller extent from deposited NH3 emissions from housing units.

For CCI, the largest impact contribution in all scenarios can be traced back to the GHG emissions occurring in the field, which are higher for all biogas scenarios than for the baseline. In compar- ison to ow and Sep, both straw scenarios had lower CO2 and N20 emissions in the field from the application of straw-digestate. The ow scenario had the highest impacts in the field, due to the high carbon content of the organic waste and because co-digestion did not result in sufficient degradation of the OM. St-field and Sep were the only biogas scenarios which performed better than the baseline in terms of CCI potential, although in the Sep scenario this advan- tage was less pronounced (4% better). St-field achieved the largest savings from the specific avoided processes, -100 kg CO2-eq, which were a result from not leaving straw in the field to degrade. Due to the scenario specific processes for St-burn, the savings for this sce- nario were not as striking. The marginal electricity that must be produced, as a consequence of not incinerating the straw, results in impacts 50% higher in terms of CCI potential in St-burn com- pared to St-field. The most important savings in all biogas scenar- ios occur from the scenario specific processes, which accounted for 25-50% of the overall savings. The second most important savings were a result of avoided CO2 and N20 emissions from application and production of mineral N fertilizer, which for the biogas scenar- ios resulted in higher savings than in the baseline scenario, by 11% in the St-field and St-burn scenarios, 22% in the ow scenario, 37% in the Sep scenario. Storage of slurry yielded considerable impacts in the baseline scenario, but not in the biogas scenarios, reflecting the storage CH4 and CO2 emissions that can be saved by digesting slurry. Impacts from additional transport related to biogas produc- tion were not important.

When biogenic C is excluded, the relative differences between the biogas scenarios and the baseline become even larger. The rel- ative benefits of St-field and Sep are then 223% and 63% larger and ow is then 54% more burdensome than the baseline. The scenarios maintain their absolute rank in relation to each other and to the baseline, with the exception of St-b, which yields larger impacts than the baseline when biogenic carbon is included, but yields impacts comparable to the baseline scenario when biogenic carbon is excluded.

There were very small variations for all biogas scenarios in com- parison to the baseline in terms of FE, with the exception of the Sep scenario, which induced 47% more FE than the baseline. The straw scenarios induced a savings to FE, caused by the straw's low P con- tent and additional savings were observed for St-field due to the scenario specific processes.

All biogas scenarios, except for Sep, performed poorly with regards to TA and PMF contributions in comparison to the baseline. However, the differences with the baseline were still of minor pro- portions, ranging from 0 to 16% for PMF and 0 to 14% for TA. As can be observed in Fig. 2, the life cycle stages contributing the most to these two categories are housing with the biggest impact, which is the same for all scenarios, followed by field application and lastly storage.

In this category, the most important impacts occur in the field from NO3 leaching and to a smaller extent from deposited NH3 emissions from housing units.

For CCI, the largest impact contribution in all scenarios can be traced back to the GHG emissions occurring in the field, which are higher for all biogas scenarios than for the baseline. In compar- ison to ow and Sep, both straw scenarios had lower CO2 and N20 emissions in the field from the application of straw-digestate. The ow scenario had the highest impacts in the field, due to the high carbon content of the organic waste and because co-digestion did not result in sufficient degradation of the OM. St-field and Sep were the only biogas scenarios which performed better than the baseline in terms of CCI potential, although in the Sep scenario this advan- tage was less pronounced (4% better). St-field achieved the largest savings from the specific avoided processes, -100 kg CO2-eq, which were a result from not leaving straw in the field to degrade. Due to the scenario specific processes for St-burn, the savings for this sce- nario were not as striking. The marginal electricity that must be produced, as a consequence of not incinerating the straw, results in impacts 50% higher in terms of CCI potential in St-burn com- pared to St-field. The most important savings in all biogas scenar- ios occur from the scenario specific processes, which accounted for 25-50% of the overall savings. The second most important savings were a result of avoided CO2 and N20 emissions from application and production of mineral N fertilizer, which for the biogas scenar- ios resulted in higher savings than in the baseline scenario, by 11% in the St-field and St-burn scenarios, 22% in the ow scenario, 37% in the Sep scenario. Storage of slurry yielded considerable impacts in the baseline scenario, but not in the biogas scenarios, reflecting the storage CH4 and CO2 emissions that can be saved by digesting slurry. Impacts from additional transport related to biogas produc- tion were not important.

When biogenic C is excluded, the relative differences between the biogas scenarios and the baseline become even larger. The rel- ative benefits of St-field and Sep are then 223% and 63% larger and ow is then 54% more burdensome than the baseline. The scenarios maintain their absolute rank in relation to each other and to the baseline, with the exception of St-b, which yields larger impacts than the baseline when biogenic carbon is included, but yields impacts comparable to the baseline scenario when biogenic carbon is excluded.

There were very small variations for all biogas scenarios in com- parison to the baseline in terms of FE, with the exception of the Sep scenario, which induced 47% more FE than the baseline. The straw scenarios induced a savings to FE, caused by the straw's low P con- tent and additional savings were observed for St-field due to the scenario specific processes.

All biogas scenarios, except for Sep, performed poorly with regards to TA and PMF contributions in comparison to the baseline. However, the differences with the baseline were still of minor pro- portions, ranging from 0 to 16% for PMF and 0 to 14% for TA. As can be observed in Fig. 2, the life cycle stages contributing the most to these two categories are housing with the biggest impact, which is the same for all scenarios, followed by field application and lastly storage.

42: The environmental impact potentials within the most relevant impact categories, through each life cycle stage, are shown in Fig. 2 for all scenarios. The net impact can be obtained by subtract- ing environmental savings (negative bars) from burdens (positive bars).

The biogas scenarios performed decisively better than the base- line for the FD and ME impact categories, while for the remaining categories, the performance against the baseline depended on the co-substrate used. FD results showed the largest net benefits for the straw scenarios, which substituted the largest quantity of nat- ural gas. At the same time, fossil fuel consumption in St-burn for the avoided incineration of straw and subsequently induced pro- duction of marginal electricity, lead to considerable losses to these benefits. The same was the case for ow and the avoided incinera-

tion of waste therein, which together with higher fuel consump- tion for heating the digester and pre-treatment of this co-substrate, made the ow scenario the least preferable of the biogas scenarios in terms of avoided FD. Moderate savings in the Sep scenario reflect the low rate of natural gas substitution arising from this co-substrate's low methane potential. However, the remaining life cycle of Sep did not induce any major fossil fuel con- sumption, which means that this scenario performs better than OW, regardless of low methane yields. There were large savings for ME of 205% for St-field, 130% for St-burn, 138% for OW, and 135% for Sep, in comparison to the baseline. In this category, the most important impacts occur in the field from NO3 leaching and to a smaller extent from deposited NH3 emissions from housing units.

For CCI, the largest impact contribution in all scenarios can be traced back to the GHG emissions occurring in the field, which are higher for all biogas scenarios than for the baseline. In compar- ison to ow and Sep, both straw scenarios had lower CO2 and N20 emissions in the field from the application of straw-digestate. The ow scenario had the highest impacts in the field, due to the high carbon content of the organic waste and because co-digestion did not result in sufficient degradation of the OM. St-field and Sep were the only biogas scenarios which performed better than the baseline in terms of CCI potential, although in the Sep scenario this advan- tage was less pronounced (4% better). St-field achieved the largest savings from the specific avoided processes, -100 kg CO2-eq, which were a result from not leaving straw in the field to degrade. Due to the scenario specific processes for St-burn, the savings for this sce- nario were not as striking. The marginal electricity that must be produced, as a consequence of not incinerating the straw, results in impacts 50% higher in terms of CCI potential in St-burn com- pared to St-field. The most important savings in all biogas scenar- ios occur from the scenario specific processes, which accounted for 25-50% of the overall savings. The second most important savings were a result of avoided CO2 and N20 emissions from application and production of mineral N fertilizer, which for the biogas scenar- ios resulted in higher savings than in the baseline scenario, by 11% in the St-field and St-burn scenarios, 22% in the ow scenario, 37% in the Sep scenario. Storage of slurry yielded considerable impacts in the baseline scenario, but not in the biogas scenarios, reflecting the storage CH4 and CO2 emissions that can be saved by digesting slurry. Impacts from additional transport related to biogas produc- tion were not important.

When biogenic C is excluded, the relative differences between the biogas scenarios and the baseline become even larger. The rel- ative benefits of St-field and Sep are then 223% and 63% larger and ow is then 54% more burdensome than the baseline. The scenarios maintain their absolute rank in relation to each other and to the baseline, with the exception of St-b, which yields larger impacts than the baseline when biogenic carbon is included, but yields impacts comparable to the baseline scenario when biogenic carbon is excluded.

There were very small variations for all biogas scenarios in com- parison to the baseline in terms of FE, with the exception of the Sep scenario, which induced 47% more FE than the baseline. The straw scenarios induced a savings to FE, caused by the straw's low P con- tent and additional savings were observed for St-field due to the scenario specific processes.

All biogas scenarios, except for Sep, performed poorly with regards to TA and PMF contributions in comparison to the baseline. However, the differences with the baseline were still of minor pro- portions, ranging from 0 to 16% for PMF and 0 to 14% for TA. As can be observed in Fig. 2, the life cycle stages contributing the most to these two categories are housing with the biggest impact, which is the same for all scenarios, followed by field application and lastly storage.

43: Between the parameters analyzed, the ones able to change the model's results considerably were; the emission factors of N20 and NH3 in the field and CH4 leaks from AD and upgrading facili- ties. When the N20 emission factor was varied in the field, the ranking of the Sep scenario changed relative to the other biogas scenarios and to the baseline (Fig. 4) When the emission factor was set high, the Sep scenario became worse than both the base- line and St-burn scenarios in both CCI and CCE impact categories. The remaining scenarios maintained their initial ranking and this ranking was also unchanged when the N20 emission factor was set low. The magnitude of the climate change impacts was much larger for all scenarios when this emission factor was set high. Changes to the biomethane potential of the co-substrates influ- enced climate change results, but to a much smaller extent. Sce- nario ranking for the FD category remained unchanged when

BMP was varied. The St-burn scenario proved sensitive to changes to CH4 leakage, becoming less burdensome than the baseline in terms of CCI and CCE, when CH4 leaks were set low. The Sep sce- nario changed ranking to the baseline when leaks were high, becoming more burdensome in terms of CCI contributions, but not in terms of CCE contributions (Fig. 4). Replacing NPK fertilizers with slurry and not only N fertilizer (data not shown) did not cause any major changes to the results, with the exception of FE poten- tial, which is essentially zero when mineral P fertilizer application is avoided in the field.

Changes to the NH3 emission factor did not affect the scenario ranking for the TA or PMF categories, with the exception of the Sep scenario, but it did increase the TA potential from the initial result by 8-18% when high and reduced it by 28-38% when low. The Sep scenario changed ranking relative to the baseline, showing less impacts when NH3 emissions were set low and vice versa when set high. The ME category was also affected by changes in NH3 emissions. The scenarios avoid more ME when NH3 emissions were set high, since this reduces the remaining N that is available for leaching and the opposite when NH3 emissions were set low. Sep changed ranking relative to the other biogas scenarios in the ME category, becoming the most burdensome when NH3 emissions were set low (see Fig. 5).

46: In general, the assessment shows that biogas production is beneficial for the environment compared to managing slurry in a

conventional way. However, this result depends heavily on the co- substrate used and on the alternative use of the co-substrate i.e. the scenario specific processes. The assessment found that using straw, which would have otherwise been left on agricultural land, is the option leading to the largest benefits and lowest burdens throughout the impact categories. Straw has important character- istics like low N and P content, low water content and high biom- ethane potential. These characteristics, lead to the largest benefits in the CCI, CCE, FD and ME categories, small FE savings, and small TA burdens. The low macronutrient content of straw allowed for the lowest field emissions after straw-digestate application, while its low water content lead to low energy consumption during heat- ing of the AD reactor. The St-field scenario also resulted in the larg- est savings due to the scenario specific processes, which allowed for large CO2 savings and savings of surplus N emissions to the environment. Other LCAs of biogas production, have also found advantages for CC in comparison to conventional manure manage- ment, but the benefits of the study at hand are more modest. As in the present study, Hamelin et al. (2014) finds less savings for a similar scenario of household waste and more savings for similar straw and separation scenarios. However, the net savings for the straw scenario in Hamelin et al. (2014) are of 143 CO2-eq, while here the net saving are 28 CO2-eq for CCE. For a similar separation scenario De Vries et al. (2012) and Hamelin et al. (2011) found an improvement from the baseline of 120% and 15%, respectively, while the savings in this study were of 63% for CCE and 4% for CCI. The more modest CC savings seems reasonable, considering the additional savings implemented in these studies from substitu- tion of P and K mineral fertilizers, combustion of the produced biogas for AD heating needs, lower fugitive CH4 emissions from AD (1-2%) and end use of biogas, which when combusted in CHP for energy recovery, leads to higher savings (Hamelin et al., 2011; Patterson et al., 2011).

Discrepancies between CCI and CCE results were observed for the biogas scenarios. These are due to a difference in the CH4 impact factor for biogenic or non-biogenic emissions in the ReCiPe method (Goedkoop et al., 2009). The differences were visible in the uncertainty analysis, which shows that when CH4 leaks from AD are high, the Sep scenario has higher impacts than the baseline for CCI, but has savings for CCE with respect to the baseline scenario. The ow scenario also performs better than the baseline in CCE, when fugitive CH4 emissions are low, but has higher impacts than the baseline scenario in CCI. However, keeping in

mind the uncertainty range shown in Fig. 4, the most important conclusion that can be drawn from the CH4 uncertainty analysis is that a definitive improvement for all scenarios can be achieved by keeping these fugitive emissions as low as possible. Strong focus should thus be placed on controlling these emissions in order to ensure benefits of using the SF or ow diverted from incineration for biogas production.

The assumption regarding the scenario specific processes of each co-substrate proved crucial and largely determined the rank- ing of the scenarios. The scenario specific processes result in large CO2 and some N20 emissions savings for the CCI category for St- field, from straw that would have otherwise been left in the fields to decay. Also, there is no additional fossil fuel consumption from this life cycle stage for St-field, such as those present for St-burn and OW. When incineration of straw and organic household waste are avoided in the St-burn and ow scenarios, it translated into large contributions to FD, CCI and CCE, from induced production of marginal electricity. Due to the energy-producing nature of the straw in St-burn, some of the benefits observed in St-field can- celled out, since the avoided production of electricity is subse- quently produced from coal. There was more coal based electricity production for St-burn than OW, because of the higher LHV per kg of straw, compared to the LHV per kg of waste. This reveals that co-substrates diverted from energy production pur- poses will have limited advantages, due to avoided electricity pro- duction that will have to be produced anyway and most likely from more burdensome fuels. The higher the LHV and lower the water content of a substrate, the more incineration is favored and vice versa for anaerobic digestion (Clavreul et al., 2012). This holds true when comparing the impacts from the scenario specific processes of St-burn and ow (Fig. 2), where St-burn had the highest impacts for CCE and the least savings for CCI. Generalizations about incin- eration and anaerobic digestion should thus be exercised with cau- tion as the complex relationship to energy of a co-substrate will depend on many factors, such as LHV, TS content, and energy source substituted, among others.

The advantages observed for all biogas scenarios in terms of ME potential were a result of two factors; the amount of mineral fertil- izer substituted and the differences in nitrate leaching factors between the digestates and undigested slurry. Ammonium nitrate application has the highest NO3 leaching factor after field applica- tion, followed by slurry and finally digestate (Table 2). The larger the amount of ammonium nitrate substituted, the more savings

of ME. The biogas scenarios substitute more ammonium nitrate than the baseline, since these scenarios have a higher N content from the co-substrates' added N and a higher MFE substitution rate. The results for this category proved sensitive to the NH3 emis- sion factor, but scenario ranking remained unchanged. This seems to point out that the biogas scenarios' benefits compared to the baseline are robust for this category. However, as stated in Moeller and Stinner (2009) there have been mixed results regard- ing the effects of digestion on N leaching, with some finding more leaching from digestate than from slurry while others finding less. The present results should thus be interpreted with caution, as the parameters determining the fate of mineral N are difficult to model with accuracy, especially in LCA models, typically relying in less complex fate models than those applied in risk assessment. This is further exemplified by the variations in the results found in sim- ilar LCAs of biogas, which show lower impacts (Hamelin et al., 2011) or impacts of equal level to the baseline for ME (De Vries et al., 2012). The variations can most often be traced back to sce- nario assumptions and modeling approaches.

On the other hand, the TA and PMF impact categories, which are dominated by NH3 emissions, showed higher impacts for the bio- gas scenarios than the baseline, except for Sep. The Sep scenario was the scenario most sensitive to changes in NH3 emissions. Depending on whether NH3 emissions were set low or high, Sep changed ranking in the ME category with the other biogas scenar- ios and switched role from being the most impactful to the least impactful scenario in the TA and PMF impact categories (Fig. 5). The benefits in TA and PMF occur as a result of the avoided appli- cation of the SF in a recipient farm and subsequently avoided NH3 emissions. The NH3 emissions from the avoided SF at the recipient farm, cancel out the effects of the extra NH3 emissions from the SF part of the digestate, essentially leaving the N from the functional unit for any other N emission. This N emission dualism results in that the Sep scenario will easily change ranking with the baseline, depending on the NH3 emission rate. In other LCAs of biogas, SF-digestate has also proven beneficial in terms of TA (De Vries et al., 2012; Hamelin et al., 2014). Furthermore, PMF and TA are two of three impact categories dominating the damage indicating endpoint results, which means the Sep scenario presents an oppor- tunity to mitigate these important emissions. However, the mitiga- tion of one impact category should not compromise benefits in another, as the Sep scenario adds the most additional N to the field, it is also the scenario that becomes most burdensome for CCI and CCE, when a high N20 emission factor is set. This seems to point out that Sep has a propensity to become burdensome if the right conditions present themselves.

Scenario assumptions were also crucial in relation to FE poten- tial and can largely affect the outcome for this impact category. In this study the only factor affecting FE impacts was the P content of the co-substrates and thus Sep, which contains the P rich SF, resulted in the highest impacts, but this is a direct consequence of applying digestate to soils with a high P profile. Accumulation of P in the soil has been previously pointed out in (Hansen et al., 2006b) and should be considered carefully in LCAs of agricultural systems. Here, it was deemed a more realistic option to assume that mineral P or K fertilizer is not avoided on animal farms because these farms have received animal manures year after year. When P and K are assumed to be substituted, then the avoided leaching from mineral P will fully cancel out the leaching of dige- state P, as was observed in the uncertainty analysis (data not shown). In addition, midpoint impacts for ME and FE did not con- tribute to damage of human and ecosystem health, as shown with the endpoints. Thus, higher results for Sep at the midpoint FE do not necessarily have deleterious consequences.
While this study modeled biogas production for injection into the natural gas grid, others have modeled natural gas combustion

in combined heat and power plants running on coal (Hamelin et al., 2011; Patterson et al., 2011). The mentioned approach leads to higher savings for CC potential and FD, as the more burdensome coal is replaced for avoided electricity production. However, this choice does not allow for expressing the benefits from being able to store biogas in the natural gas grid until electricity is needed on the grid. It is more likely that in the future, biogas which can be stored and used according to demand will be preferred. Smart grid thinking, as opposed to combusting biogas at an arbitrary time, due to limited storage capacity, is likely to be more beneficial. The methodology to quantify these temporal benefits, from which LCA practitioners will benefit greatly, is just now being developed in terms of the urgency to avoid a given GHG emissions, according to its temporal location (Jorgensen et al., 2014). Further methodol- ogy development is still needed in the area of biogenic C thinking, so that the different approaches, which yield varying results, are harmonized.

(2012) and Hamelin et al. (2011) found an improvement from the baseline of 120% and 15%, respectively, while the savings in this study were of 63% for CCE and 4% for CCI. These are due to a difference in the CH4 impact factor for biogenic or non-biogenic emissions in the ReCiPe method (Goedkoop et al., 2009). The differences were visible in the uncertainty analysis, which shows that when CH4 leaks from AD are high, the Sep scenario has higher impacts than the baseline for CCI, but has savings for CCE with respect to the baseline scenario. mind the uncertainty range shown in Fig. 4, the most important conclusion that can be drawn from the CH4 uncertainty analysis is that a definitive improvement for all scenarios can be achieved by keeping these fugitive emissions as low as possible. The assumption regarding the scenario specific processes of each co-substrate proved crucial and largely determined the rank- ing of the scenarios. The scenario specific processes result in large CO2 and some N20 emissions savings for the CCI category for St- field, from straw that would have otherwise been left in the fields to decay. Also, there is no additional fossil fuel consumption from this life cycle stage for St-field, such as those present for St-burn and OW. When incineration of straw and organic household waste are avoided in the St-burn and ow scenarios, it translated into large contributions to FD, CCI and CCE, from induced production of marginal electricity. Due to the energy-producing nature of the straw in St-burn, some of the benefits observed in St-field can- celled out, since the avoided production of electricity is subse- quently produced from coal. There was more coal based electricity production for St-burn than OW, because of the higher LHV per kg of straw, compared to the LHV per kg of waste. This reveals that co-substrates diverted from energy production pur- poses will have limited advantages, due to avoided electricity pro- duction that will have to be produced anyway and most likely from more burdensome fuels. The higher the LHV and lower the water content of a substrate, the more incineration is favored and vice versa for anaerobic digestion (Clavreul et al., 2012). This holds true when comparing the impacts from the scenario specific processes of St-burn and ow (Fig. 2), where St-burn had the highest impacts for CCE and the least savings for CCI. Generalizations about incin- eration and anaerobic digestion should thus be exercised with cau- tion as the complex relationship to energy of a co-substrate will depend on many factors, such as LHV, TS content, and energy source substituted, among others.

The advantages observed for all biogas scenarios in terms of ME potential were a result of two factors; the amount of mineral fertil- izer substituted and the differences in nitrate leaching factors between the digestates and undigested slurry. Ammonium nitrate application has the highest NO3 leaching factor after field applica- tion, followed by slurry and finally digestate (Table 2). The larger the amount of ammonium nitrate substituted, the more savings

of ME. The biogas scenarios substitute more ammonium nitrate than the baseline, since these scenarios have a higher N content from the co-substrates' added N and a higher MFE substitution rate. The results for this category proved sensitive to the NH3 emis- sion factor, but scenario ranking remained unchanged. This seems to point out that the biogas scenarios' benefits compared to the baseline are robust for this category. The present results should thus be interpreted with caution, as the parameters determining the fate of mineral N are difficult to model with accuracy, especially in LCA models, typically relying in less complex fate models than those applied in risk assessment. This is further exemplified by the variations in the results found in sim- ilar LCAs of biogas, which show lower impacts (Hamelin et al., 2011) or impacts of equal level to the baseline for ME (De Vries et al., 2012). The variations can most often be traced back to sce- nario assumptions and modeling approaches.

On the other hand, the TA and PMF impact categories, which are dominated by NH3 emissions, showed higher impacts for the bio- gas scenarios than the baseline, except for Sep. The Sep scenario was the scenario most sensitive to changes in NH3 emissions. Depending on whether NH3 emissions were set low or high, Sep changed ranking in the ME category with the other biogas scenar- ios and switched role from being the most impactful to the least impactful scenario in the TA and PMF impact categories (Fig. 5). The benefits in TA and PMF occur as a result of the avoided appli- cation of the SF in a recipient farm and subsequently avoided NH3 emissions. The NH3 emissions from the avoided SF at the recipient farm, cancel out the effects of the extra NH3 emissions from the SF part of the digestate, essentially leaving the N from the functional unit for any other N emission. However, the mitiga- tion of one impact category should not compromise benefits in another, as the Sep scenario adds the most additional N to the field, it is also the scenario that becomes most burdensome for CCI and CCE, when a high N20 emission factor is set. This seems to point out that Sep has a propensity to become burdensome if the right conditions present themselves.

conventional way. However, this result depends heavily on the co- substrate used and on the alternative use of the co-substrate i.e. the scenario specific processes. The assessment found that using straw, which would have otherwise been left on agricultural land, is the option leading to the largest benefits and lowest burdens throughout the impact categories. Straw has important character- istics like low N and P content, low water content and high biom- ethane potential. These characteristics, lead to the largest benefits in the CCI, CCE, FD and ME categories, small FE savings, and small TA burdens. The low macronutrient content of straw allowed for the lowest field emissions after straw-digestate application, while its low water content lead to low energy consumption during heat- ing of the AD reactor. The St-field scenario also resulted in the larg- est savings due to the scenario specific processes, which allowed for large CO2 savings and savings of surplus N emissions to the environment. Other LCAs of biogas production, have also found advantages for CC in comparison to conventional manure manage- ment, but the benefits of the study at hand are more modest. As in the present study, Hamelin et al. (2014) finds less savings for a similar scenario of household waste and more savings for similar straw and separation scenarios. However, the net savings for the straw scenario in Hamelin et al. (2014) are of 143 CO2-eq, while here the net saving are 28 CO2-eq for CCE. For a similar separation scenario De Vries et al. (2012) and Hamelin et al. (2011) found an improvement from the baseline of 120% and 15%, respectively, while the savings in this study were of 63% for CCE and 4% for CCI. The more modest CC savings seems reasonable, considering the additional savings implemented in these studies from substitu- tion of P and K mineral fertilizers, combustion of the produced biogas for AD heating needs, lower fugitive CH4 emissions from AD (1-2%) and end use of biogas, which when combusted in CHP for energy recovery, leads to higher savings (Hamelin et al., 2011; Patterson et al., 2011).

Discrepancies between CCI and CCE results were observed for the biogas scenarios. These are due to a difference in the CH4 impact factor for biogenic or non-biogenic emissions in the ReCiPe method (Goedkoop et al., 2009). The differences were visible in the uncertainty analysis, which shows that when CH4 leaks from AD are high, the Sep scenario has higher impacts than the baseline for CCI, but has savings for CCE with respect to the baseline scenario. The ow scenario also performs better than the baseline in CCE, when fugitive CH4 emissions are low, but has higher impacts than the baseline scenario in CCI. However, keeping in

mind the uncertainty range shown in Fig. 4, the most important conclusion that can be drawn from the CH4 uncertainty analysis is that a definitive improvement for all scenarios can be achieved by keeping these fugitive emissions as low as possible. Strong focus should thus be placed on controlling these emissions in order to ensure benefits of using the SF or ow diverted from incineration for biogas production.

The assumption regarding the scenario specific processes of each co-substrate proved crucial and largely determined the rank- ing of the scenarios. The scenario specific processes result in large CO2 and some N20 emissions savings for the CCI category for St- field, from straw that would have otherwise been left in the fields to decay. Also, there is no additional fossil fuel consumption from this life cycle stage for St-field, such as those present for St-burn and OW. When incineration of straw and organic household waste are avoided in the St-burn and ow scenarios, it translated into large contributions to FD, CCI and CCE, from induced production of marginal electricity. Due to the energy-producing nature of the straw in St-burn, some of the benefits observed in St-field can- celled out, since the avoided production of electricity is subse- quently produced from coal. There was more coal based electricity production for St-burn than OW, because of the higher LHV per kg of straw, compared to the LHV per kg of waste. This reveals that co-substrates diverted from energy production pur- poses will have limited advantages, due to avoided electricity pro- duction that will have to be produced anyway and most likely from more burdensome fuels. The higher the LHV and lower the water content of a substrate, the more incineration is favored and vice versa for anaerobic digestion (Clavreul et al., 2012). This holds true when comparing the impacts from the scenario specific processes of St-burn and ow (Fig. 2), where St-burn had the highest impacts for CCE and the least savings for CCI. Generalizations about incin- eration and anaerobic digestion should thus be exercised with cau- tion as the complex relationship to energy of a co-substrate will depend on many factors, such as LHV, TS content, and energy source substituted, among others.

The advantages observed for all biogas scenarios in terms of ME potential were a result of two factors; the amount of mineral fertil- izer substituted and the differences in nitrate leaching factors between the digestates and undigested slurry. Ammonium nitrate application has the highest NO3 leaching factor after field applica- tion, followed by slurry and finally digestate (Table 2). The larger the amount of ammonium nitrate substituted, the more savings

of ME. The biogas scenarios substitute more ammonium nitrate than the baseline, since these scenarios have a higher N content from the co-substrates' added N and a higher MFE substitution rate. The results for this category proved sensitive to the NH3 emis- sion factor, but scenario ranking remained unchanged. This seems to point out that the biogas scenarios' benefits compared to the baseline are robust for this category. However, as stated in Moeller and Stinner (2009) there have been mixed results regard- ing the effects of digestion on N leaching, with some finding more leaching from digestate than from slurry while others finding less. The present results should thus be interpreted with caution, as the parameters determining the fate of mineral N are difficult to model with accuracy, especially in LCA models, typically relying in less complex fate models than those applied in risk assessment. This is further exemplified by the variations in the results found in sim- ilar LCAs of biogas, which show lower impacts (Hamelin et al., 2011) or impacts of equal level to the baseline for ME (De Vries et al., 2012). The variations can most often be traced back to sce- nario assumptions and modeling approaches.

On the other hand, the TA and PMF impact categories, which are dominated by NH3 emissions, showed higher impacts for the bio- gas scenarios than the baseline, except for Sep. The Sep scenario was the scenario most sensitive to changes in NH3 emissions. Depending on whether NH3 emissions were set low or high, Sep changed ranking in the ME category with the other biogas scenar- ios and switched role from being the most impactful to the least impactful scenario in the TA and PMF impact categories (Fig. 5). The benefits in TA and PMF occur as a result of the avoided appli- cation of the SF in a recipient farm and subsequently avoided NH3 emissions. The NH3 emissions from the avoided SF at the recipient farm, cancel out the effects of the extra NH3 emissions from the SF part of the digestate, essentially leaving the N from the functional unit for any other N emission. This N emission dualism results in that the Sep scenario will easily change ranking with the baseline, depending on the NH3 emission rate. In other LCAs of biogas, SF-digestate has also proven beneficial in terms of TA (De Vries et al., 2012; Hamelin et al., 2014). Furthermore, PMF and TA are two of three impact categories dominating the damage indicating endpoint results, which means the Sep scenario presents an oppor- tunity to mitigate these important emissions. However, the mitiga- tion of one impact category should not compromise benefits in another, as the Sep scenario adds the most additional N to the field, it is also the scenario that becomes most burdensome for CCI and CCE, when a high N20 emission factor is set. This seems to point out that Sep has a propensity to become burdensome if the right conditions present themselves.

Scenario assumptions were also crucial in relation to FE poten- tial and can largely affect the outcome for this impact category. In this study the only factor affecting FE impacts was the P content of the co-substrates and thus Sep, which contains the P rich SF, resulted in the highest impacts, but this is a direct consequence of applying digestate to soils with a high P profile. Accumulation of P in the soil has been previously pointed out in (Hansen et al., 2006b) and should be considered carefully in LCAs of agricultural systems. Here, it was deemed a more realistic option to assume that mineral P or K fertilizer is not avoided on animal farms because these farms have received animal manures year after year. When P and K are assumed to be substituted, then the avoided leaching from mineral P will fully cancel out the leaching of dige- state P, as was observed in the uncertainty analysis (data not shown). In addition, midpoint impacts for ME and FE did not con- tribute to damage of human and ecosystem health, as shown with the endpoints. Thus, higher results for Sep at the midpoint FE do not necessarily have deleterious consequences.
While this study modeled biogas production for injection into the natural gas grid, others have modeled natural gas combustion

in combined heat and power plants running on coal (Hamelin et al., 2011; Patterson et al., 2011). The mentioned approach leads to higher savings for CC potential and FD, as the more burdensome coal is replaced for avoided electricity production. However, this choice does not allow for expressing the benefits from being able to store biogas in the natural gas grid until electricity is needed on the grid. It is more likely that in the future, biogas which can be stored and used according to demand will be preferred. Smart grid thinking, as opposed to combusting biogas at an arbitrary time, due to limited storage capacity, is likely to be more beneficial. The methodology to quantify these temporal benefits, from which LCA practitioners will benefit greatly, is just now being developed in terms of the urgency to avoid a given GHG emissions, according to its temporal location (Jorgensen et al., 2014). Further methodol- ogy development is still needed in the area of biogenic C thinking, so that the different approaches, which yield varying results, are harmonized.
